steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Persad and colleagues’ conclusion has two parts: (1) concentrated precipitation triggers more irrigation, and (2) the size of that irrigation increase is highly sensitive to an area’s baseline precipitation concentration. The correct choice must show data from both baseline scenarios — somewhat concentrated and evenly distributed — so the contrast in irrigation increase is visible.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — If baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, the amount of water being used for irrigation will increase 0.4% for surface water and 0.9% for groundwater, whereas the amount of water entering aquifers will increase 11.0% if baseline precipitation is evenly distributed.: Mixes irrigation data from the concentrated-baseline scenario with aquifer data from the even-baseline scenario. The conclusion is about irrigation sensitivity, not aquifer changes, so this comparison is off-target. ✗
  • Choice B — If baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, water use for irrigation will increase only slightly, whereas it will increase 9.0% for surface water and 7.9% for groundwater if baseline precipitation is evenly distributed.: Presents irrigation data from both baseline scenarios side by side, showing a large contrast that directly illustrates how sensitive the irrigation increase is to the starting baseline. ✓
  • Choice C — If baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, the amount of water entering aquifers will increase 4.9%, while the amount being used for irrigation will increase 0.4% for surface water and 0.9% for groundwater.: Shows only the concentrated-baseline scenario and includes aquifer data the conclusion does not address. The contrast between baseline scenarios needed to demonstrate sensitivity is absent. ✗
  • Choice D — If baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, water use for irrigation will decline by a small amount, whereas it will increase 11.0% for surface water and 9.0% for groundwater if baseline precipitation is evenly distributed.: Contradicts the table, which shows only positive (increasing) values for irrigation. No data support a decline. ✗
That matches Choice B — If baseline precipitation is somewhat concentrated, water use for irrigation will increase only slightly, whereas it will increase 9.0% for surface water and 7.9% for groundwater if baseline precipitation is evenly distributed..
